ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general coffee beverage class; no specific entry for instant 2-in-1 premixed black coffee with sugar. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers coffee and coffee substitutes broadly; no specific code for instant 2-in-1 premixed format. Closest defensible category used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34731007 refers to coffee as a substance; no specific code for instant premixed 2-in-1 format. Used as closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet dissolved in approximately 200 ml of hot water, yielding roughly 200 g of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: 2 in 1 Black Coffee (Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for '2 in 1 black coffee (sugar)' (cr_id D09010001). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 with documentation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for instant 2-in-1 black coffee with sugar as a prepared beverage. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database standard thresholds: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70) based on the GI of sucrose (table sugar, GI ~65) and the dilution effect of the beverage format, consistent with sugar-sweetened beverage analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (55 × 9.56 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 10.5. This is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement is recommended for higher-confidence classification.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (7.88 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite very low sodium (8 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened beverage and switch to unsweetened black coffee (kopi o kosong), as AHA 2018 guidelines identify added sugar in beverages as an independent contributor to elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.88 g sugar/100 g and only 0.44 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial blood glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened liquid with a GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 10.5 per serving, which will cause rapid blood glucose elevation; the ADA recommends eliminating all SSBs from the T2D diet and substituting unsweetened coffee or water.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should replace this sugar-sweetened coffee with unsweetened black coffee, as habitual SSB consumption is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 7.88 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and counteracts any anti-inflammatory benefit from coffee polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should switch to unsweetened black coffee to retain polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load that exceeds the RA dietary sugar threshold.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 2 in 1 Black Coffee with Sugar is an ultra-processed instant beverage product popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It consists of two components pre-blended in a single sachet: instant coffee powder and granulated sugar, with no added creamer or milk. The product is prepared by dissolving the sachet contents in approximately 200 ml of hot water. The resulting beverage is a clear to dark-brown, mildly bitter, sweet black coffee drink with a thin body and no creaminess. Per 100 g of prepared beverage, it provides 42 kcal, 0.47 g protein, 0.29 g fat, 9.56 g carbohydrate (of which 7.88 g is sugar), 0.44 g dietary fibre, and 8 mg sodium. The caloric density is low, but the sugar content is high relative to the beverage volume, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to rapid glucose absorption from dissolved sugar with minimal fibre buffering. It is also a concern for hypertension management because AHA guidelines identify SSBs as independently raising blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite the very low sodium content of 8 mg/100 g. For cardiovascular health, habitual SSB consumption is associated with dyslipidaemia and elevated CVD risk. The product is halal, vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free. It is commonly consumed at breakfast in Singapore and is widely available in convenience stores, coffee shops, and supermarkets. The unsweetened variant (kopi o kosong) is the clinically preferred alternative for health-conscious consumers.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09010001), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and consistent. GI is an analogous estimate documented with approved-source basis; confidence for GI component is medium, but overall record confidence is high due to exact HPB match for nutrient data.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB for this food item; this is a confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry (D09010001) and is consistent with the negligible iron content expected in an instant coffee-sugar beverage. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food. GI value of 55 is classified as LOW per University of Sydney GI Database standard thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); this corrects the previous iteration's erroneous 'medium' classification. GI is an analogous estimate based on sucrose GI and SSB dilution effect from the University of Sydney GI Database; a direct measurement is recommended. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 9.56 × 2) / 100 = 10.5.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 fix — q4: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low'. GI value of 55 is at the upper boundary of the low category per University of Sydney GI Database standard thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes to explicitly state the classification threshold. Updated glycemic_load_per_serving from 10.6 to 10.5 for arithmetic precision.

Iteration 3 fix — q8: Added 'Er He Yi Hei Ka Fei (Han Tang)' to flat aliases array to ensure complete consistency with aliases_by_language romanized entry. Performed full self-check of all aliases_by_language strings against flat aliases array; all 11 strings confirmed present verbatim.
Updated notes field to document the gi_category correction and revised GL calculation.
Updated health_context type2_diabetes guidance to reference corrected GL value of 10.5.
